speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: Hi. I'm Sandy Rebitzer, interviewing Judy Straus in Flossmoor, Illinois, on December 3, 1995. Hi. Could you start this morning by telling us your name, please, and spelling it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judy Straus., J-U-D-Y S-T-R-A-U-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ursel, U-R-S-E-L, Levy, L-E-V-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 1st, 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where will you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dortmund, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you state the name of your family, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. My father's name was David. My mother's name was Alma. And she was born a Falkenstein. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her parents, my grandparents on, on my mother's side, are Moses Falkenstein and Fina Falkenstein. And she had three brothers-- Ludwig, Julius, and Eric. My mother-- my father's mother's name was Hanchen. And his father's name-- my grandfather on that side-- was Loeb. My father had one sister. Her name was Regina, or Gina.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm married. You want to know this part now? Who lived with you when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born the day after Hitler came to power.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date on that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from what I gathered, it was the end of January, 199-- 1933. And my father immediately lost his job. And he was employed by the German counterpart of Commonwealth Edison. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so rather than looking for other work, he decided to-- that we should immigrate to Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was not very long in Dortmund. It was very brief. And my mother and I stayed with my maternal grandparents in a small town called Montabaur while my father went to Holland to set up a business. He became-- he set up an independent business.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother stayed-- this is all of what I'm told-- my mother stayed with me when I was a baby for a while. Then she moved-- went to Holland to set up housekeeping. And I stayed with my maternal grandparents until I was a year and a half. And then my grandmother brought me to Holland. So then we lived in Amsterdam until the camp started in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language were you speaking at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would presume-- I don't remember that much-- that my parents must have spoken a lot of German with each other. But they also did speak Dutch with an accent. And I went to Dutch schools. So my main language was Dutch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you like as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the pictures that I have from when it was a child, I smiled a lot. I remember feeling very lonely. I remember-- I, I'm an only child. And I just remember grownups forever talking, planning, and sort of leaving me out of it. And I just have this pervasive feeling of loneliness.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any favorite toys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I liked to read. I still have some of my books in Dutch. I liked to do jigsaw puzzles. I remember there was a And I liked to play with a doll that I got. And I-- there was some playing with, with other children. But I don't remember that as a much time-consuming activity. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had some friends who lived in the neighborhood. And obviously, we must have played together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your house like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of the time, it was like a zoo. When my parents moved to Holland until my father's business was running well, my parents took in renters. So we had different people living like in every room. I remember there was a little dog I was scared of. Then as the rest of our family-- some of the family who escaped Germany-- came through, they stayed with us. Some went to America. Some stayed with us. And my mother's brother lived with us ongoing-- before the war, during the war, after the war. My grandfather lived with us for awhile. I mean, it was really a house that was just buzzing. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your room like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I must have slept with my parents for a long time in a crib. Because I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] having measles and still being in that crib. And I think I was about four at the time. So-- because there was a shortage of bed space. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I had a room of my own with-- it was a long, sort of narrow room. Part of it was all for toys. And the other part was a unique kind of a bed that we had there that's sort of like a-- an indoor bed. It comes down, folds down. And you can have books on top. It's encased. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The house was two-- it was an apartment, but it was two stories. The bedrooms were upstairs. And the living area and the kitchen were downstairs. I actually remember that at one point when, when some of the [? razzias, ?] these-- where they, where they picked up the Jews from their homes-- my mother went through a period where she couldn't do stairs. Somehow or other, must have made her legs very weak to go to bed and not know what was going to happen during the night. But there was a full flight up to the bedrooms. We lived there from 1934 on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a time after the war broke out where-- excuse me-- the Germans made all the Jews first move to Amsterdam. Then they made them move into a certain quarter of Amsterdam, like a ghetto. And at that point, we moved. And we only were in that new place for three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to school, went to a Montessori school, went to a, a kindergarten, went to grammar school. And again, during the war, the Germans decided that all Jewish kids could, could not attend school with non-Jewish kids. So I had to shift to a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do I remember about school? I liked, I liked learning. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have, once the war broke out, some painful memories, especially once the, the, the picking up of the Jews and sending them on somewhere started. I used to be scared of going to school, because I didn't know what I would find when I came home, whether I'd find my parents. I also remember that the classroom population changed. There'd be empty seats and more empty seats. And it was never talked about. Teacher never addressed the issue of the empty seats. But-- so there was an awful lot that wasn't talked about. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember-- excuse me, again-- I remember that my grandfather and I had sort of a very significant relationship. . Because as the adults were making plans how to address survival issues, they kept me out. They kept my grandfather out. And he used to bring me to school. He'd picked me up. And I remember having a lot of consolation from being with him. It was an odd age span, I mean, because he was, like, 74 at the time. And I was five or six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's family-- very, orthodox. We had a kosher home. My father's family was not religious. He went to synagogue on the high holidays, I think, because-- out of respect to my mother. I don't remember him going on Shabbat. But the rest of us did. My mother blessed me on Friday nights. And it was a very religious home. I remember the seder took five hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was, it was all in Hebrew. And I usually ended up in tears. I mean, this is one of the only times that I remember crying. Melodies used to do something to me. I don't know why, although now I think that it had something to do with songs my grandmother sang to me when I was a baby. And then the singing stopped, because she went back to Germany. And I stayed in Holland. And my mother didn't have time to sing. So I, I think that singing, maybe especially if it's in a minor key, used to affect me emotionally. And I remember usually ending up in tears. Although you know, if after five hours, when you're a little child, maybe you're tired. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We, we kept, I mean, every single one of the holidays. Children went to youth service. I remember also sitting with my mother in, in synagogue. And all the men were downstairs on the high holidays. They wore high hats.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blue. [LAUGHTER] I don't remember. I had a lot of my clothes made for me. And I remember some flower dresses. And I remember some blue knitted dresses. But I, I couldn't give you really details. I remember I once had a, a coat with a matching hat, which I didn't like. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the war broke out, I remember one anecdote. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All Jewish people-- no, all German people-- when the, when the Germans invaded Holland, anybody who was German or came from Germany was not allowed out. They had to stay in the house, because maybe they wanted to make sure that there were no-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nobody planted. This was during the time when Holland hadn't lost yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to play outside. I was seven at the time. And there was another child playing outside. And she said, I'm German. And I said, I'm German, too. And then she says, no, you're not. Do you know the German-- I mean, she spoke Dutch. Do you know the German word for this, the German word for that? And I knew the words. And then she still was sort of quizzical. She was a little older than I was. And then she said, who's going to win this war? And so I said, I don't know. And she says, you're not German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went home with that story. And my mother said immediately, I don't want you to have anything to do with them anymore. And later on, it came out that they were traitors. You know, they gave up names of Jews and stuff like that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Out of my class in, in this, the Jewish school, there is only one other survivor that I remember meeting after the war. And there is one-- now a woman, a girl at the time-- her name was Mikka Wolf-- who I heard was-- there was an exchange program in Bergen-Belsen at one time. And I heard a rumor that she was among the exchange. But I have not been able to verify that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your life begin to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think really, it influenced how my parents dealt with things from '33 on. So this was right from the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child, when were you aware of these changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: As families started coming through on their way to America, I became aware of, you know, something that wasn't stable. Because after all, why would all these family members move? It really started coming on strong after the war broke out in 1940. Because pretty soon, there, there were edicts that you couldn't go-- you, you weren't allowed to own a business. My father's business was taken away. And he started teaching in a school. You couldn't-- as a child, you weren't allowed to go to the swimming pools anymore. Then you couldn't take street cars. Then you had to give up your bike. And it was slowly one thing after another was taken away. So you almost learned to live expecting something else to be taken away. And it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what that was like as a kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: It almost became part of, of life. It was just a scary existence all the time. I don't-- I really don't remember that much happiness. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember joyful celebrations, except they made a fuss for my birthday. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your birthday like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember that my mother had some sort of a, a decoration that pulled out. And then she had it on the buffet. And she had my presents on the buffet. And I remember that as being nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of presents did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got puzzles. I got a lot of books. I remember coloring pencils. And then I remember-- this is sort of a vital thing or a pivotal thing-- a doll. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got this doll. I don't remember how old I was. But it was a, a beautiful doll. And I sort of wrecked her. Because I insisted on feeding her. And it loosened the porcelain paint on the back of-- because the water stayed in the doll and then soaked through. I remember that I cut her hair and then tried to even it out. And it got shorter and shorter. So it was the kind of a doll that only the owner could love. And I, I did play a lot with that doll. We were not allowed out at all hours. You know, there, there was a certain time where you had to be in the house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I, I really don't remember anything joyful. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sorry, but that's the time it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned a ghetto. When were you taken to this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved-- OK. There was a period-- and I don't know why it started-- where I started dreaming that I had to go underground. And I mentioned this dream to my mother. And my mother said, that's not such a bad idea. And the way she responded, I got very suspicious. And then my mother admitted that, yes, they wanted me to go underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been in 19-- early 1943. And they weren't going to go underground. At that point, a lot, a lot of Jewish people had already been transported out of Amsterdam. And my mother said, your dad and I can-- we're young. We're strong. We can survive these work camps. They were considered-- they were portrayed as work camps. But you can't. And [? Dada, ?] my uncle, cant. And so we think it would be a good idea if you go underground. And I must have really, you know, created an unbelievable fuss. And it was postponed over and over. And then finally, my mother said, you know, it's really time. You can stay here till after my birthday, which was June 20th, 1943. And it was on, on her birthday that in the morning, the, the, the Germans, the Nazis, came with loudspeakers through the ghetto and closed it off and said everybody had to be ready for transport. So there was no escape. My mother and father wanted me to go. We had, on the veranda, like a cold bin. They wanted me to go in there. And they said in the morning I'd be picked up by the people who were going to take me to the underground home. And I said, no. I could not tolerate that. And in a way, I now think that my mother couldn't tolerate the thought, either. And so a lot of ladies came. And they helped my mother sew stars, the Jewish star, back on, on my clothes. Because it had already been removed. You know, I, I guess I was packed to go. We moved into the ghetto three weeks before this pickup. So in other words, the very end of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the first camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: To the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything-- at that point, we were allowed to move furniture and all. A lot of our good stuff had already disappeared, like the crystal, and the china, and the silver, and whatever else my parents found very valuable had gone underground with employees of my father. And they were also the ones who were going to have the hiding place for, for me and for my uncle. I could take my, my toys along. I don't remember what my room looked like in the ghetto. But it was a regular apartment. It was just in a neighborhood that wasn't of our choosing. It's still not a good neighborhood. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember going to school there. I mean, it was a real fuzzy period, those three weeks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we left on June 20th, we had like a, a backpack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we had a, like a shoulder purse made out of canvas, which was, you know, a smaller size, and a blanket roll. And that was almost standard garb for going to a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you pack in those things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly what I packed, what my mother packed for me. Because one thing that I still have is a book that went with me to all the camps. I don't know how it got out of the second camp, Theresienstadt, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the book is the only thing that I remember really taking. My doll had gone underground, because my parents recognized that this was a valuable item for me. So my doll was hidden, and also some of my toys. In fact, in, in our kitchen here, I don't know that you noticed, there's some blue toy dishes. Those are-- they were hidden during the war. And we got them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the roundup came, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were marched for hours to a, a station in Amsterdam. And then went-- and people were still dressed in, in those days. I remember my father wore a hat. Then we were marched on a train. And I don't remember whether this was a, a people train or cattle train. I really do not remember that. And then we were taken to a camp in Holland, a transit camp called Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through some sort of an official registration procedure that took all day and part of-- we arrived there during the night. And then it took all the next day. So we were not-- we were sort of processed. And I can't tell you exactly what happened there, but you had to give up your jewelry and stuff like that. I do remember that my parents had given me some sort of a little leather case that was under the clothes. And there was something in it. And I don't remember exactly what it was. But I was scared about it being discovered. I really do not remember. I remember the looks of the case. And I don't know where it is. It's-- I certainly don't have it now. And in this process, and the kids were sort of shunted aside. I don't even remember eating or drinking or anything like that. And then we were assigned barracks by evening. And the women's barracks were all full. And I remember my mother and I shared a third floor bunk in the men's barracks and held on for dear life, both figuratively and also physically. Because it was a very narrow bunk and way up there. Although, we later on chose third floor bunks because-- third level bunks-- because then you don't have the hay of the mattress above you raining on you. It was more practical. There was also less stealing on the third level bunks than on the lower level, because people couldn't take anything. There was stealing in camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you got up to this third level bank? What-- do you remember anything of your first days there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. We were assigned then to a regular barrack. Women and children were separate from men. My father-- wait a minute. This camp was originally started by the Dutch who set it up for people, for Jewish people who came from Germany who didn't have any place to go. And they didn't want to send them back to Germany. So they created this little village, so to speak. And then when the Nazis came in, they just surrounded it. They surrounded it with barbed wire, with guard houses, and a moat, which was sewage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then it became a concentration camp, a transit camp, in Holland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- I, I was sick a lot. My father-- wait a minute. My father got work. Some of the people who were originally in this camp-- there was one couple my mother knew. And through them, she was able to get my father introduced to the, the camp department that did all the electrical work. And that was my father's profession.
